planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in warm soil; a white-skinned pumpkin that makes a striking alternative to orange jack-o'-lanterns for fall decoration and has sweet, edible flesh that works well in baking.

directions: Sow seeds 1 inch deep after last frost in warm, fertile, well-amended soil, spacing hills 5–6 feet apart; Lumina is a vigorous vine type that needs plenty of room and full sun. Lumina is the most widely grown white pumpkin, producing round, 10–20 pound fruits with a smooth, creamy-white to ghostly-white exterior rind that makes it one of the most sought-after decorative pumpkins for Halloween and fall displays; the interior flesh is orange, sweet, and of good quality for cooking and pies — better than many orange carving pumpkins. The white skin provides an excellent canvas for painting, engraving, or decorative carving. Harvest when the rind is hard and the skin resists puncture with a thumbnail; cure for 1 week and store in a cool, dry location for 2–3 months.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in deeply amended soil; the 'Cinderella pumpkin' of legend — deeply ribbed, brilliant red-orange, and flat like a coach wheel — sold at Parisian markets since the 1880s and still considered one of the finest pumpkins for French cuisine.

directions: Sow 1 inch deep after last frost in enriched, well-drained soil, hills spaced 6–8 feet apart; thin to 2 plants per hill once true leaves appear. Rouge Vif d'Etampes — 'vivid red of Etampes,' the French market town where it was sold — is a century-old French heirloom pumpkin famous for its dramatically flattened, deeply ribbed, vivid red-orange form that resembles the coach from the Cinderella fairy tale; fruits weigh 15–35 pounds and have brilliant orange, fine-grained, dry, sweet flesh of outstanding quality for soups, gratins, and tarts in the French culinary tradition. Cure for 2 weeks at warm temperatures after harvest and store in a cool, dry place for up to 4 months; the unusual shape makes it a dramatic autumn centerpiece as well as an excellent culinary pumpkin.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in warm, well-amended soil; a compact, single-serving size blue-grey squash in the Hubbard family — ideal for gardeners who want the exceptional Hubbard flavor in a size manageable for a single meal.

directions: Sow seeds 1 inch deep after last frost in warm, composted soil; Blue Ballet is a compact vine type needing 4–6 feet of space, much more manageable than giant Hubbard types while retaining the same outstanding eating quality. Blue Ballet is a small, 2–4 pound, blue-grey teardrop-shaped winter squash in the Cucurbita maxima (Hubbard) family, with dense, smooth, bright orange flesh that is extraordinarily dry, sweet, and nutty — essentially the same outstanding Hubbard flavor in a size perfect for a single serving or small family portion that eliminates the wrestling match of cutting a 15-pound Blue Hubbard. Excellent for roasting halved in the oven with butter and maple syrup; the small size also makes it practical for stuffing. Cure for 10 days and store for 3–4 months.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in warm soil; an All-America Selections winner with brilliant yellow, scallop-edged pattypan fruits and bushy, compact habit that produces prolifically over a long season in hot climates — well-suited to Gulf Coast spring and fall gardening.

directions: Sow seeds 1 inch deep after last frost in warm, fertile soil; Sunburst is a bush-type pattypan that requires no support and takes up significantly less space than vining squash. Sunburst is an All-America Selections award-winning summer squash producing vivid golden-yellow, flying-saucer-shaped pattypan fruits with slightly scalloped edges and tender, mild, buttery white flesh that is best harvested at 3–4 inches when the skin is still soft and the seeds are immature; left to grow larger the skin toughens and flavor diminishes. The compact bush plants are highly productive in hot weather and are excellent for Gulf Coast gardens where the fast harvest window allows planting in both spring and early fall. Sunburst is virtually identical to Bennings Green Tint but with consistent bright yellow rather than pale green coloring.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in warm, fertile soil; cousa is the traditional squash of Lebanese, Syrian, and Egyptian cooking — fat, pale green, and slightly sweet, it is the classic squash for stuffing (mahshi kousa) and is exceptionally heat-tolerant for zone 9–11 production.

directions: Sow seeds 1 inch deep in warm soil after last frost; cousa squash is a compact bush type that produces prolifically even in hot weather, making it well-suited to the Gulf Coast and zone 8–11 summer conditions where European zucchini often fails under humidity and heat stress. Cousa is a traditional Middle Eastern summer squash with fat, club-shaped to slightly bulging, light-green to pale-grey-green fruits harvested at 4–6 inches for the classic Levantine stuffed squash dish (mahshi kousa), where the flesh is hollowed out, filled with seasoned rice and meat, and braised in tomato broth; the compact interior, firm flesh, and mild, sweet flavor make it superior to standard zucchini for stuffing as it holds its shape better. Also excellent sliced and sautéed or grilled.
